name: Name from Lucena — circular through Rute
name_original: Benamejí desde Lucena — circular por Rute
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Lucena are set amidst a vast "sea of olive trees" and within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park, offering diverse terrain. The region features a mix of challenging ascents, smooth asphalt roads, and flat sections that wind through valleys and charming villages. A notable feature is the Vía Verde del Aceite, a long greenway built on a former railway line, providing a unique cycling experience. The landscape also includes natural monuments and lakes, such as the Lagunas del Sur de Córdoba, and the Sierra de Aras, which offers panoramic views. Best road cycling routes around Lucena The most popular road cycling route is Name from Lucena — circular through Rute, a 52.7 miles (84.8 km) trail that takes 3 hours 53 minutes to complete. This difficult circular route features over 3,300 feet of elevation gain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puente Genil from Lucena — circular via Aguilar de la Frontera, a moderate 54.4 miles (87.5 km) path. This route offers varied terrain through the Andalusian landscape. Local road cyclists also love the Castillo del Moral – Cabra loop from Lucena, a 55.1 miles (88.6 km) trail leading through the region's hills, often completed in about 4 hours 22 minutes. Road cycling around Lucena is defined by extensive olive groves, the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park, and the Vía Verde del Aceite. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging routes with significant elevation. The routes in Lucena are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.7 stars from 3 reviews. More than 1,800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Lucena's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of road cycling routes around Lucena?
A: Lucena offers a diverse range of road cycling routes catering to various fitness levels. You'll find everything from moderate rides to challenging routes with significant elevation gains. Out of 113 available routes, 37 are considered moderate, and 73 are difficult, ensuring options for both experienced cyclists and those looking for a demanding workout.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes with significant elevation gains in Lucena?
A: Yes, the terrain around Lucena includes challenging ascents, with some routes featuring up to 2,000 meters of elevation gain. For instance, the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20128838" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo del Moral – Cabra loop from Lucena&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 55.1-mile (88.6 km) trail with over 1,300 meters of elevation gain, leading through the region's hills.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I expect to see on road cycling routes in Lucena?
A: Road cycling routes in Lucena are set amidst a 'sea of olive trees' and within the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park. You can expect to see picturesque valleys, charming villages, and impressive 19th-century metal viaducts along the Vía Verde del Aceite. The region also features natural monuments and lakes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/guide/3822890/lakes-around-lucena" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lagunas del Sur de Córdoba&lt;/a&gt;, including Laguna Amarga and Laguna Dulce, which are important ecological reserves.

Q: Are there any scenic viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The Sierra de Aras provides breathtaking panoramic views of surrounding towns across several provinces on clear days. You can also find viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5394288" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Malpasillo Dam and Genil River&lt;/a&gt;, offering stunning vistas of the natural landscape.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Lucena?
A: Lucena, with its Mediterranean climate, generally offers good conditions for road cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn are typically ideal, providing pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather. While summer can be hot, early mornings or late afternoons can still be suitable for rides, especially on routes with shade or near water bodies.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in Lucena?
A: While many routes in Lucena are moderate to difficult, the Vía Verde del Aceite offers a unique, generally flatter experience suitable for families. This disused railway line is a 120km cycling and hiking route, providing a safe and scenic path through olive groves and over viaducts, away from vehicle traffic.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available from Lucena?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around Lucena are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1017168363/name-from-lucena-circular-through-rute" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Name from Lucena — circular through Rute&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 52.7-mile (84.8 km) loop, or the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1008811121/puente-genil-from-lucena-circular-via-aguilar-de-la-frontera" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Genil from Lucena — circular via Aguilar de la Frontera&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the Vía Verde del Aceite and how does it relate to road cycling in Lucena?
A: The Vía Verde del Aceite (Greenway of Oil) is a significant feature for cyclists in the region. It's a 120km disused railway line transformed into a cycling and hiking route, known as the longest in Andalusia. It allows cyclists to immerse themselves in the region's olive groves and history, passing over 13 impressive 19th-century metal viaducts and through repurposed railway stations, some housing interpretation centers.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Lucena?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.7 stars from 3 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse terrain, the extensive olive groves, and the unique experience of cycling through the Sierras Subbéticas Natural Park and along the Vía Verde del Aceite.

Q: Are there any specific cultural or historical sites accessible by road bike near Lucena?
A: Yes, road cycling routes can lead you to several cultural and historical sites. Along the Vía Verde del Aceite, you can find repurposed railway stations, such as the Cabra station, which houses an Olive Interpretation Center. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1109429" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Santuario Virgen de Aracelí (Lucena)&lt;/a&gt;, located on the Sierra de Aras, is a notable religious building and offers panoramic views.

Q: Are there any easier road cycling routes for beginners in Lucena?
A: While many routes are moderate to difficult, there are some easier options. Out of 113 routes, 3 are classified as easy. These routes typically feature less elevation gain and shorter distances, providing a good starting point for those new to road cycling in the region. The Vía Verde del Aceite also offers generally flat sections suitable for a more relaxed ride.

Q: Can I find cafes or places to rest along the road cycling routes in Lucena?
A: Yes, the region's routes often pass through quaint villages where you can find cafes and local establishments. Some of the repurposed stations along the Vía Verde del Aceite have also been converted into restaurants or interpretation centers, offering convenient spots for a break and refreshments during your ride.
tips: Architectural complex from the 18th century declared an Asset of Cultural Interest in 2006. The hospital now houses a nursing home. The hospital was founded at the express wish of Saint John of God (also known as John of the Sick, a Portuguese saint, nurse, and the founder of the Hospitaller Order of Saint John of God) for the care of the poor affected by contagious diseases.
It is a town famous for its anise distilleries. There is even the Anís Monument and the Anís Museum.

A beautiful town that is the beginning of the 9. stage of the Camino de Santiago - Camino Mozárabe de Málaga. Near the Ermita del Calvario there is a free hostel for pilgrims. The keys are found at the local police office.

One of the towns visited by King Felipe V in the 18th century as part of a program to learn about the country. Among the places of interest are: the Parish of the Immaculate Conception, the Hermitage of San Sebastián and the Museum of the Duchess of Benamejí (a historical tour of nineteenth-century romantic banditry).
Good place to rest, plenty of shade and benches available in this small rest area near the N-432 road between Lucena and Aguilar de la Frontera.

waypoints.name: Church and Former Hospital of San Juan de Dios
